good.summary:
The finding was correct and I confirmed the mechanism in the source before touching it. `update()` runs for both 'playing' and 'boss' (loop L871: `if(state==='playing'||state==='boss')update(dt)`), it unconditionally decrements `roundTimer` (L383), and the round-over check at L444 was ungated by state. Entering the shop is itself triggered by `roundTimer<=0`, and `startBoss()` never refreshed the timer — so the first `update()` after the Continue click re-fired the round-over branch, and because nothing on the shop->boss path increments `game.round`, `isBossRound(game.round+1)` was still true and it set state back to 'shop'. Permanent soft-lock at round 4.

Two changes, both minimal:
1. Load-bearing: gated the round-over check to `game.state==='playing'`. The boss round is designed to end via `bossDefeated()` (L324), never on the clock.
2. Companion: `game.roundTimer=ROUND_TIME` in `startBoss()`, because `drawHUD()` renders the clock during 'boss' (L774) and would otherwise show a frozen 0. This is cosmetic only — I verified it is NOT a substitute for the gate.

I also found the regression suite was partly wrong and fixed it (details in suiteResult) — it contained characterization tests asserting the buggy behavior, which would have gone green on the broken build and red on the fix.

Note for the orchestrator: there are TWO copies of this game. I edited only the one I was told I may edit (ancf-digital-projects). The copy at `games\advice-whack\index.html` is a different file (md5 e1daa6af vs b75f4dce) and is STILL UNFIXED — I left it untouched per the rules, and used it as the positive control. It is also the suite's default target when no path argument is passed, so `node test_advice-whack.js` with no args still fails. Someone should decide which copy is canonical.

good.summary:
Confirmed the finding by reading update() and reproducing it: the made-shot branch is guarded by a one-shot `scored` flag, but the out-of-bounds miss branch had no equivalent guard. After a miss, `ball`/`ballInFlight` stay live until the 500ms reset timeout fires, and the ball keeps travelling further out of bounds each frame — so the same `if` re-fired and re-incremented game.shots (and re-called endShotIfDone, re-firing sndWin/saveBest) on every update() until the timeout landed. Observed: one miss took shots 0 -> 19.

I did NOT use the finding's suggested `missed` flag, because probing revealed the finding understates the flaw. A MADE ball also falls through into the unguarded miss branch once it drops off-screen (observed shots 1 -> 27 on the made path). A separate `missed` flag leaves that broken: the made ball would still register one spurious MISS (extra shot, streak reset, MISS popup, duplicate sndWin). This bites hardest on the final shot, where endShotIfDone() returns true and the reset timeout is never scheduled at all, so nothing ever clears ballInFlight.

The honest invariant is "a shot resolves exactly once, made or missed" — one condition shared by both branches. So I took the finding's own stated alternative (reuse the existing, already-correctly-managed flag) and renamed it `scored` -> `resolved` to make the shared meaning truthful, guarding both branches with it. The flag is cleared in flick() — the only place that sets ballInFlight=true — and in resetGame(), never in the reset timeout. No behaviour is changed on the make path: the rename is a pure 4-site substitution (`scored` was read only in its own guard and never exposed on the __cfq hook).</summary>

Both HIGH findings were real; I reproduced each mechanism in the source before touching it.

FINDING 1 (pause freezes wave progression) — confirmed exactly as reported and fixed as prescribed. The wave-clear branch armed a wall-clock `setTimeout(...2000)` whose callback checked `game.state==='playing'`. Pausing during the 2s gap made that check false when it fired, so it silently no-oped; `spawning` was already false, so the wave-clear branch could never re-arm. Replaced the raw timer with a `game.nextWaveT` countdown ticked from `update()`'s own dt, immediately after the `state!=='playing'` guard — so pausing suspends the countdown and resuming continues it. I heeded the finding's explicit warning and did NOT retry the transition inside togglePause(): a 300ms pause in the gap yields 1 -> 2 with no skipped wave.

FINDING 2 (towers make no difference at the starting budget) — root cause confirmed, but THE PRESCRIBED FIX IS INSUFFICIENT, and I have receipts. The finding correctly diagnoses a fire-rate-vs-dwell-time mismatch but then only fixes the dwell side (enemy speed /10) plus damage x3, leaving the fire rates untouched. Applying its exact patch, the core assertion still fails: wave 1 with a tower is byte-identical to wave 1 with none (lives 2, score 24) — the finding's own reported symptom, unchanged. Running the real suite against that variant: 45 pass / 1 fail, the failure being finding #2's own assertion.

The arithmetic shows why. speed/10 gives a ~950ms dwell beside a straight path run; Boundary's 1200ms rate still yields <1 shot per enemy pass, so 24 damage can never clear a 60hp Peer Pressure, so no kills, so no gold, so no bootstrap. I swept the damage/rate space (36 configs) and fixed BOTH sides of the mismatch the finding named: kept speed/10 and damage x3 (24/54/18), and divided all three fire rates by 4 (1200->300, 1800->450, 800->200), preserving their relative identity. 300ms lands ~3 shots per pass = 72 damage, just clearing the 60hp threshold. Both surviving guards (towerStats' 180ms floor, towersUpdate's 120ms floor) are unaffected.

I also corrected two now-false comments in the suite that asserted the tower economy was unfixable, and one that claimed 2 towers were placed where the code places 1.

good.summary:
Confirmed the finding's mechanism first-hand. checkTyped() normalizes both sides with .replace(/[^a-z0-9 ']/g,''), which strips the closing period that all 12 responses end with. So the prefix missing only the final '.' normalizes equal to the target — I verified by enumeration that EVERY response has exactly two matching prefixes (e.g. "That's not something I'm planning." matches at length 33 AND 34). checkTyped() runs from the #ti 'input' listener on every keystroke, so one finished sentence fired the full correct-answer branch twice: double score, double streak, and two 350ms nextPrompt() timers. nextPrompt() unconditionally does current=queue[game.idx]; game.idx++, so the second call silently skipped a prompt the player never saw — a "10 prompt" round ended after 5 answers.

Minimal fix, three parts:
1. A module-level one-shot `resolved` flag, cleared in nextPrompt() and startRound(), gating checkTyped().
2. Require typed.length >= current.r.length alongside the normalized compare, so the match fires on the last keystroke rather than one keystroke early. I deliberately deviated from the finding's suggested remedy here: it proposed keeping the early match and swallowing the trailing period in the input listener, but the existing suite asserts "the response is not marked solved until its final character (the period) is typed" — the guard alone would leave that check failing. Requiring full length is the actual root fix, matches the game's own stated rule ("Each character must match"), and made the finding's step-5 input-swallow unnecessary (nextPrompt() already clears typed/ti.value on hand-off). Punctuation leniency is preserved where it can matter (e.g. typing '-' for the em dash still matches).
3. Gated the timeout path with the same flag.

good.summary:
Confirmed the finding: the 60% win target was unreachable — 8 crystals could only ever paint ~3% of the canvas, so every playthrough ended on "Garden Stalled" and the win screen was dead code. But the finding's prescribed fix was partly wrong: raising the branch cap 12->60 is INERT, because the fork RATE (rng()<s*0.8 = 0.8/sec over a crystal's ~9s life) caps a crystal at ~6 branches — the cap of 12 was never reached. Verified empirically (38 branches across 8 crystals = 4.75 each, matching the rate, not the cap); applying the finding verbatim still lost 5/5 at ~4% peak.

Fix (minimal, in the file's terse style): (1) fork rate 0.8 -> 6/sec, which is what actually binds and which makes the 60-branch cap meaningful (~45 branches/crystal); (2) branches now fork from a random existing branch's CURRENT tip via per-branch ox/oy offsets rather than always from the crystal origin — starburst -> area-filling growth; (3) maxLen/speed buffed per the finding; (4) TARGET_FILL 0.60 -> 0.10.

Two extras the finding missed. First, a losing ending must survive: once 8 seeds reliably won, "Garden Stalled" risked becoming as dead as the win screen was, so I tuned against BOTH endings — open-space play wins 19-20/20, edge/corner play (growth wasted off-canvas) stalls 15-18/20. That gap is the game's actual skill. Second, a latent score bug the fix exposed: update() re-derived game.score from coverage on the line right after calcCoverage() won the round, clobbering the time/efficiency bonuses in the same frame (win panel read "Score: 10" beside "Best score: 34"). Unreachable while the game was unwinnable; live the instant I fixed the win. Guarded with if(game.state==='playing').

good.summary:
Confirmed the finding by reading the code. RINGS is declared smallest-radius-first (index 0 = Clarity r=30/50pts ... index 4 = Awareness r=200/5pts), but the hit-test loop in throwDart() scanned it backwards, starting at the LARGEST ring and breaking on the first `d<=RINGS[i].r` match. Since almost any on-board hit satisfies d<=200, the loop matched Awareness on its first iteration every time and never looked at the smaller, higher-value rings nested inside it. Every on-board dart scored a flat 5 points and was labelled "Awareness", so the rings, colours and per-ring point labels drawn on the board were purely cosmetic and aiming had no effect on score.

Fix: reverse the scan direction so it walks inward-out (i=0 upward). Because RINGS is sorted by ascending radius, the first containing ring found walking outward IS the innermost one, so the smallest/highest-value ring correctly wins. No exclusive band bounds are needed. Added a two-line comment stating the ascending-radius ordering the loop depends on, since that invariant lives 90 lines away at the RINGS declaration and silently breaks the scoring if anyone reorders the array.

good.summary: The finding was correct and I confirmed the mechanism myself before editing. carveMaze() links two "room" cells that sit 2 apart, which means it must open TWO shared edges: origin<->corridor and corridor<->destination. Each of the four direction branches cleared only three of the four wall flags involved — it never cleared the corridor cell's FORWARD wall. Because movePlayer() only reads the wall of the cell the player currently occupies, every carved edge became one-way: you could step from a room into the corridor, but the corridor's forward wall still blocked you from leaving it. Result: from the spawn only 1 step was ever possible and the exit was unreachable on every level — the game could not be completed even in principle. The fix adds the missing corridor forward-wall clear to all four branches (4 lines), leaving the destination-side write intact so the shared wall data is symmetric from both sides. That symmetry is exactly what makes movePlayer()'s cheap single-cell check correct, so no change to movePlayer() or the renderer was needed.

The soft-lock is real and I reproduced it: a firefly is retired forever (f.missed=true) the first time its blink timer flips it on->off without a catch, but the round only clears at game.collected>=12 — so one missed blink makes the round mathematically uncompletable. My probe: a good-faith bot resolved all 12 flies in round 1 (10 caught, 2 missed), leaving ZERO flies in play, yet round=0/state='playing' after 8,000 further ticks.

I did NOT apply the finding's suggested one-liner (clear the round when every fly is collected-or-missed). I built it, measured it, and rejected it: because every fly self-retires within ~2s regardless of the player, it turns the win condition into a stopwatch. Measured on that build, a player who NEVER MOVES THE MOUSE reaches "All Lit! ✓" in 11.5s, and the game-over path becomes nearly unreachable (you win before blobs can kill you). That breaks normal play, which the brief rates worse than the bug.

Instead I fixed the actual root cause, one step deeper: f.missed=true is the guard-that-refuses-a-legitimate-action (PROTOCOL's canonical bug shape) — it retires a firefly after a SINGLE blink, in a game whose whole premise is that fireflies blink repeatedly. Now a blink-off uncaught is a scored missed OPPORTUNITY (game.missed / roundStats.opportunities still increment, so accuracy is unchanged), never a retirement. Only f.collected removes a fly from play, so 12/12 is always reachable and the win check needs no change. Head-to-head: idle player -> lives=0, never wins; skilled player -> won, caught 96/96. Bonus: the HUD "Collected: n/12" and the won-panel "Every firefly found." stay truthful, which the finding's fix would have falsified.

The suite was ALSO wrong and I fixed it (see suiteResult) — it asserted the soft-lock as expected behaviour and passed the broken build 54/54.

good.summary:
The finding was correct and I confirmed the mechanism by reading before touching anything. A round is defined as PER_ROUND=20 *collected* stars (HUD reads "Collected: x/20", title says "3 rounds of 20"), and the only code that completes a round required game.collected>=20. But the spawn gate counted misses against the round's spawn budget: (uncollected+collected+misses)<20. So once collected+misses hit 20, no star could ever spawn again — with 1..7 misses the player could never reach 20 collected (round can't complete) and never reach MAX_MISSES=8 (gameover can't fire). The round became permanently unresolvable: HUD frozen, clicks dead, no ending. Missing even one star in a round was enough.

I took the finding's recommended gate fix rather than the tempting alternative of completing the round on collected+misses>=20 — that alternative unlocks the game but silently redefines a "round of 20", letting a player finish at 17/20 while the HUD still reads 17/20 and reach the win screen with fewer than 60 stars, contradicting the game's own title text. The gate fix keeps a round honestly worth 20 collected and still guarantees termination, because every spawned star must end as either a collect (drives collected toward 20) or a miss (drives misses toward 8).

Two changes, both minimal, no refactor or restyle: the gate itself, and deletion of the dead branch that concealed the bug. I added a comment on the gate stating the invariant (misses must not consume the spawn budget) and why (otherwise neither ending is reachable) rather than naming the bug.

One note worth flagging: the regression suite did NOT fail on this bug — it passed 60/60 on the broken build because its "THE FINDING" section asserted the soft-lock as expected behaviour ("SOFT-LOCK: state never leaves playing", expecting round===0 forever). That is a suite encoding the bug as the spec, so it could never have caught a regression. I rewrote that section to assert the correct behaviour (a round with 3 misses still spawns all 20, still advances) rather than weakening anything, and verified it is now a real positive control: it fails the pre-fix build and passes the fixed one.

good.summary:
The finding was correct and I confirmed the mechanism in the source before changing anything. die() increments game.levelDeaths, then — on the non-terminal branch — calls buildLevel(game.level) to set up the retry. buildLevel() unconditionally does game.levelDeaths=0 (line 55) as part of rebuilding a level. So die() erased the counter it had just incremented, every single time. levelDeaths could never exceed 1 mid-flight and always read 0 by the time die() returned, making the `>=MAX_DEATHS_PER_LEVEL` check mathematically unreachable: infinite retries, a HUD "Lives" readout frozen at 3/3, and a "Clean streak" that credited levels the player had just died on. The whole death-limit/game-over ending was dead content.

The fix preserves the tally across the retry rebuild inside die() only — one line, no refactor. I deliberately chose this over the tempting alternative (deleting the reset from buildLevel), because buildLevel's reset is *correct* for every other caller; removing it would push the burden onto three fresh-level call sites and silently break two of them. Keeping the reset where it belongs and making the one exceptional caller (a retry is not a fresh level) restore its own state is the change with the smallest blast radius.

I also had to correct the regression suite. Beyond the one intentionally-failing assertion, five of its assertions pinned the buggy behavior as the specification (expecting levelDeaths===0 after a real death, and asserting cleanStreak increments on a level the player died on). The suite was also internally contradictory: it demanded deathsObserved===6 AND gameover within 6 deaths, which cannot both hold since its own loop stops the moment gameover fires — it could not go green under either the buggy or the correct behavior. I inverted those assertions to assert correct behavior and added positive coverage (clean level still credits the streak; a real gameover is now driven end-to-end rather than force-set, since its "gameover is unreachable" rationale is now obsolete). This strengthens the suite — verified below.

The finding was correct and I confirmed the mechanism independently: `game.pendingAdvance` was read (line 201) and cleared (line 202) but never assigned `true` anywhere in the file. Since it was always `undefined`, the body of `advanceIfDone()` — the only code that increments `game.hIdx`, calls `buildHaiku()` for the next haiku, or sets `game.state='won'` — could never execute. Solving a haiku scored points and played the win chime, then the board froze forever; 7 of 8 haikus and the entire win screen were unreachable.

Fix (4 lines, no refactor, matching the file's terse style):
1. `checkDone()` now sets `game.pendingAdvance=true` alongside `game.correctAnim=800` — the flag the advance path waits on.
2. `advanceIfDone()` is now called every frame in `update()` rather than only inside `if(game.correctAnim>0)` — it is already self-guarded by `correctAnim<=0 && pendingAdvance`, so this is a no-op when nothing is pending, but it means a pending advance can no longer be stranded if it misses its single frame.
3. `pendingAdvance:false` is now declared on the game literal, and reset in `buildHaiku()` next to `game.correctAnim=0`, so no restart can inherit a stale `true`.

Comments state the invariant that must hold (why the flag must be false on a fresh board; who consumes it), not the bug history.

good.summary:
Rounds 4-5 spawned pieces at a random rot of 0/90/180/270, tryPlace only accepted rot===0, and nothing in the file ever mutated rot — so 13/16 and 14/16 pieces were permanently unplaceable and the game soft-locked in round 4 forever. Confirmed the mechanism by reading the source (rot is referenced at only 5 sites: assigned at 76/77, gated at 89, drawn at 124/129) and by driving the real listeners.

Fix: added the missing rotate control. A press with no drag (<4px) on a rotation round rotates the piece 90deg. Works for mouse and touch with no new UI, and needs no change to tryPlace's existing gate. A rotate is deliberately neither a move nor a miss, so perfect rounds (moves===pieces.length) stay reachable once rotation unlocks.

I did NOT apply half the suggested fix, and this is deliberate. The finding called dropping the `*0` on line 129 "ALSO REQUIRED". It is the opposite of required — it would defeat the feature. Line 129 counter-rotates the label about the SAME pivot as line 124, so removing the `*0` forces every label upright. The tile is a square with a symmetric diagonal gradient, so its own rotation is near-invisible; "label reads upright" is the player's ONLY cue for rot===0, the one state that snaps. Enabling that line would make rotation invisible and the puzzle unsolvable by sight. I removed the dead no-op instead and left a comment stating the constraint so nobody re-enables it.

Found and fixed a SECOND real bug that the finding's own suggested onUp shape would have shipped: a piece rejected for its rotation stays lying exactly on its target, so any later "drag home" is a zero-distance gesture that reads as another tap. The player rotates it upright and it sits on its outline looking correct but refuses to snap until jiggled. The rotate path now completes a resting piece, guarded so tryPlace is only called when certain to accept (an unguarded call would score a rotate as a miss and silently reset the combo streak).

Also flagged, not fixed: test_jigsaw-lite.js defaults to __dirname/../games/jigsaw-lite/index.html, a stale Jul-1 copy inside the tests project, NOT the real game. It must be run with an explicit path. I left that copy untouched; it now works as a positive control.

good.summary:
The finding was correct and I reproduced it before changing anything. Both deferred transitions in kite-soar were scheduled with setTimeout and opened with `if(game.state!=='playing')return;`. Because setTimeout runs on wall-clock time but the guard tests game state, pausing anywhere inside the window meant the timer fired while paused, hit the guard, and silently dropped the transition — with nothing to ever reschedule it. Two player-visible symptoms: (a) collect the 15th star then pause -> round never advances, HUD frozen at 15/15 forever; (b) lose the last life then pause -> game stays "playing" with 0 lives, a zombie state.

Fix: drive both transitions from the game loop instead of the wall clock. Added `advanceT`/`advanceRound`/`gameoverT` countdowns to `game`, ticked at the top of `update()` right after the existing `state!=='playing'` early-return. That early-return is what makes it correct: while paused the countdowns simply stop ticking, so a pause now DEFERS a transition instead of dropping it. The countdowns resume exactly where they left off.

Ordering is preserved deliberately: `gameoverT` ticks first and returns, so a pending round-advance can never land on top of a gameover — that is the race the original paired guards existed to prevent, now enforced structurally rather than by a state test.

A third confirmed bug fixed itself as a side effect: grabbing a bonus star inside the transition window used to schedule a SECOND timeout and double-credit the life bonus (score 420 vs the correct 405). Re-setting `advanceT` just resets one countdown, so it can only fire once. That check now passes without being touched.

I also had to fix the regression suite (see suiteResult) — it encoded the buggy wall-clock model.

good.summary:
The finding's mechanism is real and I reproduced it. Winning writes a save with total>=WIN_TARGET (maybeWin -> saveGame). resetGame() zeroes total for a fresh round but then unconditionally calls loadGame(), which restores that winning total straight back. Since total only ever grows, the very next click re-trips maybeWin() and the game instantly re-declares victory.

I fixed it one level deeper than the finding suggested, in loadGame() rather than at the call sites: a save whose total already crossed WIN_TARGET represents a FINISHED run and must never be resumed, so loadGame() now returns early and leaves the caller's fresh state in place. The round's first saveGame() overwrites the stale win, so no localStorage.removeItem is needed and loadGame stays side-effect-free.

I chose this over the suggested resetGame(fresh) patch for two reasons. First, it is smaller: one guard instead of edits at three call sites. Second, and decisively, the suggested patch is INCOMPLETE — it keeps the title branch as bare resetGame(), so a player who wins, reloads the page, and clicks the title screen still resumes the winning save and still instant-re-wins. I reproduced that path (PATH B below) before fixing. Guarding inside loadGame() closes all three entry points at once, because all three funnel through resetGame -> loadGame.

Lifetime best is unaffected: saveBest writes BEST_KEY, a different key this guard never touches. As a side benefit the best is now honest — the buggy build inflated it to 250001 by counting the phantom re-win click; it now reads 250000, the real score.

good.summary:
The finding was correct and I reproduced it. buildLevel(lv) sized the grid and seeded the RNG from its lv argument but never assigned it to game.level, so game.level was only ever written by resetGame() (to 1) and continueGame(). Both win-advance paths called buildLevel(game.level+1) with game.level stuck at 1, so every win rebuilt the identical seeded level-2 puzzle forever. I applied the finding's recommended fix — assign the level inside buildLevel (with a clamp), which repairs both advance paths at once — and deleted the dead tautology in the mouse path.

That fix alone was NOT sufficient, and this is the important part. Making levels 3+ reachable for the first time exposed a latent generator bug that had been hidden behind the soft-lock: level 3 was UNSOLVABLE. I proved it with a complete exhaustive search over every rotation assignment (959,094 nodes, search exhausted, not a budget cutout). Shipping only the level fix would have delivered players out of a repeating-level-2 loop straight into a dead level — a strictly worse soft-lock. Two generator defects caused it:

1. The random walk's fallback branch (line 142) checked only bounds while the main move (line 138) checked `visited` — the same guard present in one place and forgotten in the other. The fallback could step onto a visited cell, pushing a duplicate into `path`; the duplicate's exit bits then overwrote the earlier copy's, silently destroying the carved solution. Instrumenting a copy showed level 3 built on an attempt with dupCells=1.
2. buildLevel force-overwrites the source/dest masks to single fixed bits (E-only/W-only) AFTER carving, discarding the carve's exits. If the path didn't leave source eastward and enter dest from the west, the guaranteed solution was destroyed. The file's own comments claim the carve "constructively proves solvability" — it never verified this.

I fixed both by honouring `visited` in the fallback and rejecting invalid carves so the attempt reseeds. Verified across levels 1-200: every level builds within the 30-attempt cap (worst case 12), zero duplicate-cell paths, so no level silently ships unbuilt.

I also fixed the test suite's solver, which was genuinely wrong — not weakened. It enumerated all 4 rotation states without deduplicating, but rotationally symmetric pipes repeat masks (a cross has 1 distinct orientation, a straight 2). On level 3, 16 of 48 cells are straights, so it re-searched identical subtrees, doubling per cell and blowing its 100k node budget on a level I had already proven solvable. Deduping by mask searches the same space with no redundant branches — strictly more capable. Positive control per PROTOCOL: the strengthened suite still FAILS the pristine HEAD build (36 pass / 6 fail) on exactly the progression bug, so the solver change masks nothing.

good.summary:
The finding was real — all 8 puzzles were unwinnable — but its prescribed fix was incomplete, and applying it as written still left every round unsolvable. I confirmed the mechanism myself before and after each step.

Three defects had to be fixed:

1. Target hits were tested only at the beam's TERMINUS. A beam passing straight over its target scored nothing, and the mirror-hit branch of castBeam() never tested targets at all. Replaced with a segment-based hitTargets() called from BOTH branches (the finding's Fix 1 — correct).

2. Puzzle generation never guaranteed a colour was reachable. The finding's Fix 2 (put each colour's first mirror on its own beam line) is necessary but NOT sufficient, which I verified rather than assumed: with it applied, all 8 rounds STILL failed. The reason is a constraint the finding missed — the mirror's initial angle stayed `rng()*Math.PI` (continuous), while the only player control is a whole PI/8 click, which swings the REFLECTED beam by PI/4 (45 degrees). So only 8 reflected directions are ever reachable, on a randomly-offset 45-degree lattice, while hitting a 30px target ~450px away needs 7.6 degrees of precision (~17% chance per colour, and every colour must align at once). Observed: mirror on the beam line but closest approach to target 135-451px across all 16 click states. Fix: the generator now builds each colour's waypoint chain (prism -> stage 1 -> [stage 2] -> target) and derives each initial angle as `solutionAngle - off*PI/8` (off = 1..7), so the aiming angle is always an exact whole number of clicks away — wrong at the start, but always clickable to a solution.

3. Found while proving normal play (the suite cannot catch this, as it calls rotateMirror(idx) directly and bypasses the real click path): my stage-2 mirrors landed within mirrorAt()'s 32px pick radius of another colour's stage-1 mirror (16.6px apart in round 4). mirrorAt returns the FIRST mirror within 32px, so the overlapped mirror could never be rotated by a real mouse click — round 4 would still have been unsolvable for an actual player. Stage 2 now gets its own column past every stage-1 mirror plus its own y slot; minimum pairwise separation across all 8 rounds is now 63.4px.

I also fixed a stale-timer hazard surfaced by the suite: each solve scheduled a setTimeout(700) round-advance that survived buildPuzzle(), so a pending advance belonging to a deleted puzzle still ran game.round++ (the counter read 17). buildPuzzle() already tears down solved/particles/history; it now cancels this timer too.

Notes for the parent: (a) the suite's default path resolves to a SEPARATE, stale copy at "ANCF 100 HTML Games/games/prism-split/index.html", not the audited file — it must be run with the explicit path, which is what the sweep did (its evidence matches ancf-digital-projects exactly: round 0 Joy 1.1px / Freedom 53.5px). I left that default alone because all 89 sibling suites share the same convention. That duplicate is now divergent and still carries the bug. (b) I did not touch the clip math on the old line 91; the segment hit test makes the terminus cosmetic, and I verified the terminus segment always reaches the target (all aims are rightward toward x=900 < W).

good.summary:
The finding was correct and I reproduced the mechanism before touching anything. When the player crosses the finish line, `game.dist` stays >= DIST_PER_ROUND and `game.state` stays 'playing' for the full 400ms until the setTimeout callback calls buildRound() and finally resets dist. Nothing guarded the branch, so every rAF frame in that window (~24 at 60fps) re-awarded the +50/+25 bonus and queued another independent setTimeout, each of which incremented game.round and called buildRound(game.round). The round counter cascaded forward and the player skipped rounds they never played.

Fix: a `game.roundEnding` re-entry flag, set when the branch first fires and cleared at the top of the timeout callback (before buildRound/won, so buildRound's own state reset can't strand it). Also initialized to false on the game literal and reset in resetGame(). The resetGame() reset is the load-bearing half: without it a flag left true on a restart path would permanently deadlock ALL future round advancement — the block would never re-enter again, which is a worse failure than the original bug. I added a comment at the guard stating the invariant (dist stays over the line until buildRound resets it 400ms later) rather than describing the fix, and a comment on the resetGame line explaining why the second clear is not redundant.

Note for the caller: the suite's DEFAULT path resolves to a second, stale copy of the game at "games\relay-run\index.html", which was byte-identical to the real file before my edit. I ran the suite against the real target via its argv[2] parameter rather than edit that copy (outside my permitted scope). That copy is now divergent and a bare `node test_relay-run.js` will still show the 2 failures — someone should decide whether it is a build artifact to regenerate or dead weight to delete.

good.summary: CONFIRMED the finding, with one correction: it is worse than reported. spawnBubbles() gave each bubble a random horizontal sign, but update() moves bubbles with `b.x -= b.spd` and bubbles spawn at x=W+r+i*140 (>=987), already right of the arrow's live boundary (arrow dies at x>W+10, i.e. 970). So a negative-spd bubble begins unreachable and only drifts further away -- structurally unhittable, not merely hard. Because the RNG is seeded purely from the round number, this is identical on every playthrough. The finding framed this as a round-2 problem; the sign is drawn per bubble, so EVERY round is its own dice roll: 30 of 50 bubbles across all 10 rounds are unhittable (round 2 = 5/5, rounds 5/7/8 = 4/5, round 10 = 1/5). The core action ("burst the stigma bubbles") was a guaranteed no-op for 60% of the game's content, hidden by the MAX_SHOTS_PER_ROUND=8 exhaustion backstop force-closing bubbles so rounds still "completed". FIX: dropped the `*(rng()>0.5?1:-1)` sign multiplier so every bubble drifts toward the archer. Benign, expected side effect (called out in the finding): removing that rng() call shifts the seeded stream, so layouts differ but remain valid and deterministic. SUITE: the task said the suite fails on this bug; it did not -- it PASSED 63/63 on the broken build because its diagnostic asserted `unreachable === 5`, encoding the bug as expected behavior. I inverted it to assert the real invariant and extended it to all 10 rounds plus a dynamic reachability probe. This strengthens rather than weakens it: the corrected suite now fails the broken build on 3 checks (it previously failed on none). NOTE FOR THE PARENT: the suite's default path resolves to a STALE DUPLICATE of the game at "ANCF 100 HTML Games/games/stigma-archery/index.html", not the ancf-digital-projects file under fix; running the suite bare tests the stale copy and reports green regardless of the real fix. good.summary:
Confirmed the finding by reading the code. When the walker reached the far end, update() awarded score and scheduled a 400ms setTimeout to advance the round — but left game.state==='playing' and player.x/progress untouched, so every subsequent rendered frame in that 400ms window (~24 real frames at 60fps) re-entered the same `if(progress>=1)` branch, re-awarding score and scheduling another game.round++. The fall branch just above it already had an explicit `return;` guarding against exactly this; the crossing branch had nothing.

Fix is a transition lock: a module-level `transitioning` flag, set true immediately before each deferred setTimeout, checked with an early `return` at the top of update() (right after the existing state check), and cleared in buildRound(). buildRound is the correct clear point because it is the sole entry into any playable round, so it covers the round-advance callback, the fall/rebuild path, and restart via resetGame(). Clearing inside the setTimeout callback would leave the flag stuck on the `won` branch, which never calls buildRound.

Four one-line changes, no refactor, no restyle, matching the file's terse style. Two comments added stating the invariant (why the flag exists, why buildRound is the clear point) rather than naming the bug.

Note for the caller: tests/test_tightrope.js defaults to a STALE COPY of the game at "ANCF 100 HTML Games/games/tightrope/index.html", not the file under fix. Run it with the real path as argv[2] (as I did) or that copy will need syncing separately — I did not touch it, per the edit rules.

good.summary:
Both HIGH findings are the same defect and both were real: the reveal timer that auto-advances a round 900ms after an answer was never captured or cancelled, so tapping "tap to continue" advanced immediately AND the orphaned timer advanced again later; across a restart, orphaned timers landed in the fresh game and ran it to the win screen.

I fixed the root cause at the funnel point rather than at the symptom: one module-level `revealTimer` handle, a `scheduleAdvance()` helper that clears any pending timer before scheduling (so "at most one pending" is actually true, not just intended), a clear at the top of `advanceRound()` (every advance route funnels through it — tap-skip, reveal timer, timeout), and a clear in `resetGame()` so no timer survives a restart.

While proving the fix I found it was INCOMPLETE, and that the finding's own suggested patch would have MASKED a worse bug rather than fixed it. The update() countdown guard was `if(game.chosen<0)`, but `-2` is the timeout sentinel and `-2 < 0` is true — so after a timeout the whole branch re-ran EVERY FRAME of the 900ms reveal, re-scheduling a timer, re-incrementing `answered` and re-playing the buzzer ~54 times at 60fps. This is exactly the sentinel bug PROTOCOL documents for draft-reply. The finding's `scheduleAdvance()` would have cancelled each stray and hidden it; my probe caught it because I drove update() at a realistic frame rate. Measured on the unfixed build: ONE timeout inflated `answered` to 55 and blew `round` from 0 to 55, instantly ending the game — strictly worse than the reported symptom, and invisible to the audit because its harness never ran update() during the reveal. Fixed by tightening the guard to `game.chosen===-1`.

The regression suite still failed after a correct fix, and the suite was wrong — I fixed the suite, did not weaken it (details in suiteResult).

NOT consecutive... the recovery window is real, and this path needs no fix."

Sweeping 40 adversarial seeds I measured a tightest life-loss gap of 1 FRAME (16ms) — consecutive, exactly what the fixer asserted does not happen. It is not limited to deliberate stacking: with the naive alternating player, 14 of 36 gameovers lost ALL 3 lives inside 1 second (tightest span 160ms for all three; gaps of 2/3/4 frames routine), at t=20-50s.

Mechanism confirmed by a recovery probe: once |totalTorque| exceeds the spring's authority at the limit (0.28*0.25=0.0700) the equilibrium sits beyond MAX_ANGLE, and because weights are never removed, switching to PERFECT play at that instant recovers 0/5 seeds — all 3 lives burn in <1s, faster than the ~1.2-2.8s spawn interval can deliver a corrective weight. That is the original finding's exact symptom ("no second weight even having spawned yet to attempt a correction"), moved from t~1s to t~20-50s rather than eliminated.

Mitigating: the original finding explicitly blessed the never-removed weights as acceptable post-fix, so the residue is arguably in-scope-excluded. But the fixer volunteered an affirmative all-clear on this specific path, and that all-clear does not survive a wider sweep — the fixer generalised from a single seed after correctly warning itself (quoting PROTOCOL.md) that an inconclusive probe is not an all-clear. Proof is thin on the one claim that most needed it. Verdict incomplete, not rejected: the shipped physics change is correct, well-chosen, and strictly improves the game.
needsAttention.summary:
The beam had damping but no spring. `update()` did `angleV += totalTorque*s; angleV *= 0.98`, which makes angleV decay toward a NONZERO steady state (≈49·totalTorque·s), not toward zero — so `angle` grew monotonically forever the instant any imbalance existed, and weights are never removed from the beam, making that forcing term permanent. One ordinary weight therefore ran the beam to MAX_ANGLE and held it there, re-firing the tip-over penalty until all 3 lives were gone in ~1s — before the second weight had even spawned. Meanwhile never clicking at all won for free, since zero weights meant zero torque.

I applied both fixes the finding identified as jointly necessary, and confirmed by simulation they are jointly necessary (neither alone wins):
1. `placeFalling()`: deleted the one-shot `angleV += falling.val*dx*0.00015` kick. The weight is already pushed into `weights` on the line above, so `update()`'s totalTorque already accounts for it — the kick double-counted it and was not dt-scaled (up to 0.03 rad in a single frame).
2. `update()`: added a restoring torque `angleV += (totalTorque - angle*RESTORE)*s` with `RESTORE=0.25`, giving the beam a real equilibrium at `totalTorque/RESTORE` instead of a runaway.

The finding is correct and I reproduced it from the source. Because player.x is pinned to 80 and SEG_W is 80, the very first collision check of every level resolves segAt(scroll+80) to segment index 1 — segment 0 is drawn but never collided against. buildLevel() spawned the player at a fixed y=H/2=270 regardless of where that actually-checked segment's centreline had wandered to. The tunnel is a seeded random walk starting at H/2 and the gap narrows every round (minGap=180-r*8), so at round 5 segs[1].cy=337.4 with gap=140 puts the top wall at 267.4 — the player spawns already 9.4px inside it. vy starts at 0, so one frame moves the orb ~0.08px: no input could save it. buildSegs is seeded only by the round number, so every retry regenerated identical fatal geometry, burning all 3 lives in 3 frames and hard-capping the advertised 10-cave game at cave 6.

The fix spawns the player on the centreline of the segment collision actually resolves to, with the scroll reset moved above the spawn so segAt reads the new level's scroll. This makes the spawn margin structurally gap/2 - PLAYER_R — guaranteed safe by construction rather than by luck of the seed, which matters because the seed constant, SEG_W, minGap and wobbleMul are all tunable.

I deliberately did NOT take the tempting alternative of making collision resolve to segment 0 (via player.x, SEG_W, or segAt's index math). That only reshuffles which random cy the fixed spawn is compared against; it survives the current 10 seeds by luck alone — I measured segment-0 margins of just 12.60px at round 8 and 14.67px at round 9, which would break on any retuning.

needsAttention.summary:
Confirmed the finding by reading the code. clickCell() decides a round synchronously but defers the consequences to real setTimeouts (600ms on a win, 500ms retry / 450ms gameover on a miss). During those windows nothing moved game.phase off 'input', so the click guard `state==='playing' && phase==='input'` kept accepting taps for a round that was already over. On the win path each extra tap compared idx against sequence[playerInput.length-1] — undefined, since the index is past the finished sequence — so it always mismatched and fell into the wrong-answer branch: mistakes++, streak=0, a life lost, or in endless mode an outright false game over.

The fix closes the input window synchronously in both branches with an inert phase: `game.phase='celebrate';litCell=-1;`. update() only acts on 'transition' and 'show', so an unrecognised phase makes it a no-op while the pending setTimeout owns the round; both existing timeouts already restore phase='transition' when they fire, so recovery is untouched. I deliberately did not reuse 'transition' — update() would have run transitionT past 280ms and flipped to 'show', re-flashing the OLD sequence for the rest of the window before buildRound() replaced it.

Two things worth flagging beyond the finding. (1) The bug was worse than reported: it charged one penalty per tap, not one total — five strays drove lives to -1 and mistakes to 5 (observed on the unfixed copy). (2) The regression suite did NOT fail on this bug as the task stated — it passed 84/84, because its 'bug candidate' section asserted the buggy behavior as expected ("BUG: a stray click ... is scored as a wrong answer", asserting mistakes+1 and lives-1). It characterized the bug rather than guarding against it, so it would have gone red on a correct fix. I inverted that section to assert what the player is owed, added the previously-untested miss-window path, and added a counter-assertion that a genuine mistake still ends an endless run so the fix can't make endless unlosable. That makes the suite strictly stronger, not weaker.

needsAttention.summary:
Confirmed the finding by reading the code, then fixed it. clearLines() scans the board bottom-up (r=ROWS-1 down to 0), so rowsToFlash holds full-row indices in DESCENDING order. The removal loop then does grid.splice(r,1) followed by grid.unshift(...). Those two operations do not cancel for the rows the loop still has to visit: splice removes at r, but unshift re-inserts at index 0, so every row ABOVE r shifts down by one index while rows below r keep their index. Because the loop walks downward (19, then 18, ...), each subsequent index is stale by one more each time — it ends up pointing at an already-empty row, and a genuinely full row is skipped and left welded to the board. The player gets scored for a Tetris while up to 2 of the 4 rows never leave.

Minimal fix: iterate the same indices in ASCENDING order. For rows above r the -1/+1 shifts do cancel, and rows below r haven't been visited yet, so every index stays valid. One line changed, plus a comment stating the invariant (why ascending is required, and why the sort is done on a copy) — the copy matters because game.flashRows aliases rowsToFlash and the draw overlay must keep the original pre-clear indices.

Side effect to expect, per the finding and confirmed in my run: the All-Clear bonus was previously suppressed by this bug (the isAllClear check runs after removal and could never see an empty board). It now fires — that is the designed behaviour, not a regression. Visible in the genuine-drop proof: score 1336 = 300 base + 1000 all-clear + 36 hard-drop points.
